The primary cause. When a ship travels at hypersonic speeds (roughly 7,000+ m/s), it compresses air in front of it so quickly that the air turns into superheated plasma, exceeding 3,000F. A human can jump from the tallest peak and not feel a heat. You don’t just catch on fire as a human when failing from a height.

If space starts at 100km, and he jumped at 38km, he was only a little more than a 3rd towards space but still 2/3rds from space. 
So although he was high, he jumped from within earth's atmosphere, not space.

I said yeah I did, and didn't give it much thought at the time. Couple years went by and I realized the earth is a flat level plane covered by a firmament. That dude was telling me the truth, and I didn't even engage him. I didn't ask what he meant or anything; I just dismissed him. I don't even remember what became of him after that. All I know is that he must've been a flat-earther, and that if I asked him more about what he meant that I may have saved myself a couple more years of being an idiot who thought we live on a globe ..

Red Bull really over-hyped the hell out of Felix's jump for media/advertising attention, and there was really no need at all for the "capsule" that he ascended in. That was just added dead weight, and actually prevented him from going higher. After all, Felix (like Alan Eustace here) was already wearing his "capsule" - his pressure suit. Hell, when Joe Kittinger jumped in 1960, he ascended in a "gondola", which was basically just an open seat attached to the balloon, with the spacesuit life support O2 needed for the ascent accounting for most of the added weight the balloon had to lift.
This record is quickly broken by a 57-year-old man in his spare time  with a very understated balloon and no capsule.😂😂😂😂

Joseph William Kittinger II (July 27, 1928 – December 9, 2022) was an American military pilot who was an officer in the United States Air Force. He served from 1950 to 1978 and earned Command Pilot status before retiring with the rank of colonel. He held the world record for the highest skydive—102,800 feet (31.3 km)—from 1960 until 2012.[1][2]
Excelsior III: On August 16, 1960, Kittinger made the final high-altitude jump at 102,800 feet (31,300 m).[8] Towing a small drogue parachute for initial stabilization, he fell for 4 minutes and 36 seconds, reaching a maximum speed of 614 miles per hour (988 km/h)[7][11] before opening his parachute at 18,000 feet (5,500 m). Incurring yet another equipment malfunction, the pressurization for his right glove malfunctioned during the ascent and his right hand swelled to twice its normal size,[12][13] but he rode the balloon up to 102,800 feet before stepping off.[14]

This video does not show a jump from space.
It is a high-altitude skydive from the stratosphere (~39 km), which is still within Earth’s atmosphere. That is why a parachute works.
True space begins around 100 km (Kármán line).
From orbital altitudes (~400 km, ISS), a parachute-only descent is physically impossible due to:
orbital speed (~7.7 km/s),
lack of usable atmosphere,
extreme re-entry heating.
A capsule with heat shield and controlled re-entry is mandatory.
High-altitude skydive ≠ space jump.

Humans have jumped from the edge of space to Earth, but it requires specialized equipment and conditions. Let me break it down:

🚀 Real-Life Example
Felix Baumgartner (2012): He jumped from a balloon capsule at about 39 km (24 miles) above Earth, in the stratosphere.

He wore a pressurized suit and used a parachute.

During freefall, he reached speeds of 1,357 km/h (843 mph) — faster than the speed of sound — before safely parachuting down.

⚠️ Why It’s Not Simple
Outer space begins at ~100 km (the Kármán line). Jumping from there would expose you to:

No breathable air

Extreme cold

Vacuum pressure (your blood and tissues would boil without protection)

You’d need a space suit to survive, plus a parachute system to slow down before hitting the atmosphere.

🌌 Physics of the Jump
Gravity will pull you down, but the thin atmosphere at high altitudes means you won’t slow down immediately.

As you descend, air resistance increases, eventually stabilizing your speed (called terminal velocity) before you deploy a parachute.

✅ Conclusion
You can jump from near-space with the right gear — but from true outer space (like orbit), you’d need a spacecraft to slow you down first. Otherwise, you’d burn up or crash at orbital speeds.

Of course, Rasheed Ahmed — here’s the full explanation in clear, simple English 👇


---

🧍‍♂️ Felix Baumgartner’s Jump (Red Bull Stratos Mission, 2012)

Many people think he jumped from space, but actually he did not.
He jumped from the stratosphere, which is still inside Earth’s atmosphere, not outer space.

His jump height: about 39 kilometers (128,000 feet) above Earth.

Real “space” begins at about 100 kilometers (the Kármán Line).
So he was less than halfway to space.



---

🌍 Gravity was still strong there

Even at 39 km, Earth’s gravity is almost the same as it is on the surface —
that’s why he fell back down.
People often say “there’s no gravity in space,”
but that’s not true — gravity exists everywhere, only gets weaker with distance.


---

🎥 Why the video looked like space

The air was extremely thin,

The sky looked black,

The curvature of the Earth was visible.
That made it look like outer space — but scientifically, it wasn’t.



---

✅ Summary

Question	Answer

Was he in space?	❌ No — still inside the atmosphere
Was gravity zero?	❌ No — gravity was strong enough to pull him down
Could he fall to Earth?	✅ Yes — that’s exactly what happened
Was the video fake?	❌ No — it was real, but often misunderstood



---

So, Rasheed Ahmed, your doubt was absolutely correct —
he didn’t jump from space, he jumped from the upper atmosphere.

Would you like me to make a simple diagram showing how far he was below space (the Kármán Line)?

🧍‍♂️ فیلکس بامگارٹنر کی چھلانگ (Red Bull Stratos Mission, 2012)

یہ انسان نے اب تک کی سب سے اونچی چھلانگ تھی۔

مگر وہ “خلا” (space) سے نہیں، بلکہ Stratosphere (یعنی زمین کی فضا کی دوسری تہہ) سے چھلانگ لگا رہا تھا۔

وہ زمین سے تقریباً 39 کلومیٹر (یعنی 128,000 فٹ) اونچائی پر تھا۔

خلا (space) 100 کلومیٹر سے شروع ہوتی ہے — یعنی وہ ابھی فضا کے اندر ہی تھا، خلا میں نہیں۔

🌍 کششِ ثقل وہاں ختم نہیں ہوتی

39 کلومیٹر کی اونچائی پر بھی زمین کی کششِ ثقل تقریباً ویسی ہی ہوتی ہے جیسی نیچے۔

یعنی وہ زمین کی طرف کھینچا جا رہا تھا — اسی لیے وہ واپس نیچے آ سکا۔

“خلا میں کششِ ثقل صفر ہوتی ہے” کہنا درست نہیں؛
خلا میں بھی کششِ ثقل موجود ہوتی ہے لیکن بہت کمزور۔



---

🎥 وڈیو کیوں “خلاء” جیسی لگتی ہے؟

اس لیے کہ وہاں فضا بہت پتلی تھی،
آسمان سیاہ دکھائی دے رہا تھا، اور

Several Red Bull athletes have died while participating in their sponsored sports. These deaths have occurred in BASE jumping, wingsuit flying, and climbing, highlighting the inherent risks associated with these extreme activities. 

Here are some notable examples:
Felix Baumgartner:
Austrian base jumper and skydiver renowned for his record-breaking jump from the stratosphere, died while paragliding in Italy on July 17 2025.

Shane McConkey:
A renowned skier and BASE jumper, McConkey died in 2009 during a ski BASE jump in Italy. 

Ueli Gegenschatz:
A BASE jumper and wingsuit flyer, Gegenschatz died in 2009 after a parachute malfunction during a BASE jump in Switzerland. 

Valery Rozov:
A BASE jumper and wingsuit flyer, Rozov died in 2017 during a wingsuit jump in Nepal. 

Dean Potter:
A celebrated climber and BASE jumper, Potter died in 2015 during a BASE jump in Yosemite National Park. 

Ueli Steck:
A renowned climber, Steck died in 2017 during an accident near Mount Everest. 

Matilda Rapaport:
A professional skier, Rapaport died in 2016 after an avalanche accident in Chile. 

These deaths, along with others, have prompted discussions about the relationship between sponsorship, risk-taking, and the potential dangers of extreme sports. Some have criticized Red Bull's marketing strategy, suggesting that it promotes dangerous stunts and pushes athletes to take excessive risks.

This was the biggest event I can remember in my life. I recall sitting in my Freshmen English class the day he was supposed to do the jump and it got canceled. I was sneaking my phone under the desk in a dark room (we were watching a movie in class). I was not gonna miss this. I told everyone about how cool it was gonna be.

Then the day finally came and I got to watch it at home. It was PHENOMENAL to watch live! To this day, my family still brings up how I wouldn’t shut up about this jump 😂 We had gone to Universal Studios that year and wanted to give fake names to the ET ride. I told them “Felix” cuz it was the only name I could think up at the time. Idk why I was so obsessed with it. Never wanted to be an astronaut or anything. I just knew this would be an historic occasion and it WAS!
Thank you Felix for an awesome memory!

"I Jumped From Space (World Record Supersonic Freefall)" refers to a historic event in which a person jumped from the edge of space and fell at supersonic speeds, breaking a world record. The jump was made from an extremely high altitude, around 128,000 feet (39 kilometers) above Earth. The person who did this was Felix Baumgartner, an Austrian skydiver, in 2012.

Felix wore a special space suit to protect him from the cold and lack of oxygen in the upper atmosphere. During the jump, he reached speeds faster than the speed of sound—over 1,300 km/h (800 mph)—before safely landing back on Earth. This jump set a world record for the highest freefall and the fastest fall without an aircraft. It also helped scientists learn more about the effects of high-altitude jumps on the human body.

Who told him he wasn't going to spin? Any inconsistency in a projectile's movement lends itself towards yawing and tumbling, within a medium. High velocities, shifts in medium density, external directional forces, and greater rearward mass amplify this. This is because the medium's resistance against the frontal surface slows it down, while other parts of the projectile are kept moving by inertia. If the projectile's rearward mass stays behind that which is receiving residence, it will drive it forward. Otherwise the rear will try to take the lead, with its greater energy, which can result in yawing, overcorrection and rotation.

Back in the day I was wondering why he wasn't floating if he was truly in space. He jumped from a height of approximately 128,000 feet (about 39 kilometers), which is in the stratosphere, far below the boundary of space (Kármán line) which is at about 100 kilometers (62 miles) above Earth's surface. Astronauts float in space because they are in a microgravity environment. Baumgartner, on the other hand, was still well within Earth's atmosphere and under the influence of its gravity, which is why he fell back to Earth rather than floating. Still amazing, but it's a bit of a weird brag since it's hugely inaccurate.

I think this is a tremendous achievement, and I'm not denigrating this man's accomplishment... but this "jump/freefall from space" headline is erroneous and misleading.
To jump from SPACE, proper, is not possible - my "skepti-sense" was straight off the scale upon seeing the title of this video, alone. I watched it and was so convinced of dishonesty and illegitimate that I had to do some research... because that's rather an easily disproven - and unbelievably audacious claim, even by today's standard of the internet rendering truth and fact utterly irrelevant!
The truth of the situation - that this man took a balloon up into the highest levels of the earth's atmosphere (specifically, the stratosphere) and freefalled... exponentially more plausible, and lends a significant amount of credence to the video as a whole.
Now, that is a record-breaker, with reports putting him about 60km away from the 'edge' of the atmosphere - A.K.A. outer space.
Close? Yes. Courageous, of course, I wouldn't jump from a fraction of that height. 
But, space does not a stratosphere make... and it is that misleading embellishment that I take issue with. I find it unlikely that the jumper somehow was ignorant of this distinction, that nobody who aided him in this feat at any point clarified what was actually happening - but it takes nothing from the bad-assery of the act.
It simply is the difference between being regarded as credible, and thus being admired - 
or being dismissed as an attention-seeking fool who is apparently so ineffectual as to be incapable of even lying competently, and disregarded entirely.
I caution those who will accept as fact any claim so incredible... try to verify, backup, or at least know whether you even KNOW if it could be BS or not, BEFORE you mindlessly regurgitate it to the world.

it is not possible to frame the entire Earth from an altitude of 40 km. To understand why, let's do a simple calculation:

Earth radius: Approximately 6,371 km
Distance from the object to be photographed: 40 km
Considering the Earth as a perfect sphere, the distance between the observer (at an altitude of 40 km) and the farthest point on the Earth's surface is given by the formula:

Distance = Earth radius + Observer height

In this case:

Distance = 6,371 km + 40 km = 6,411 km

Edward Hendrie has said:

President Bush said that NASA would start with unmanned missions. ‘’beginning no later than 2008, we will send a series of robotic missions to the lunar surface to research and prepare for future human exploration.’’ Bush planned for the first manned mission to the moon in 2015.

 2015 has come and gone, and there has been no NASA manned missions. Why not? It would seem easy enough. Just dust off the old Apollo program plans and start right up again. The rockets and landers supposedly worked perfectly on six separate moon missions 40 years ago, with much older technology. Going back to the moon, with today’s technology, should be a piece of cake.

 It seems that NASA cannot replicate the Apollo missions and is therefore forced to go back to the drawing board. Why? One reason is that NASA and its contractors allegedly lost the plans for the Lunar Excursion Module (LEM) and the Lunar Rover. They also claim that they lost the plans for the huge engines that powered the Apollo spacecraft.

 Don Pettit is a NASA astronaut who is a veteran of several long duration missions aboard the mythical International Space Station. In 2016, Pettit was speaking at the Space for Innovation Conference at London’s Science Museum, where he stated: ‘’I’d go back to the moon in a nanosecond. The problem is that we don’t have the technology to do that anymore. We used to, but we destroyed that technology. And it’s a painful to build it back again.’’

 When there are allegations that an event was faked, and evidence that could prove that it actually happened disappears while it is in the possession of the perpetrators of the alleged fraud, the only reasonable conclusion is that the missing evidence would prove the fraud.

 Obviously, the plans were made to disappear, because those plans would reveal the impossibility of landing on the moon. NASA and their contractors would rather appear to be incompetent knuckleheads, than be indicted for criminal fraud, so they just ‘’lost’’ the plans that could prove the fraud.

 NASA is good at taking great studio photographs of the astronauts and producing elaborate patches to memorialize its missions for posterity. However, it can’t seem to maintain the really important records of its alleged achievements. Aaron Ranen discovered during production of his documentary of the Apollo moon missions that all the audio tapes from the Apollo missions have also disappeared.

No, it is not possible for a human to break the sound barrier by free falling from space. The sound barrier is the term used to describe the sudden increase in aerodynamic drag that occurs as an object approaches the speed of sound. In Earth's atmosphere, the speed of sound is approximately 343 meters per second (1,125 feet per second) at sea level.

When an object, such as an aircraft, approaches the speed of sound, it experiences a rapid increase in drag, which can cause turbulence and other aerodynamic effects. Breaking the sound barrier requires a significant amount of thrust and specialized design to overcome these effects.

While it is true that Felix Baumgartner, an Austrian skydiver, performed a high-altitude jump from the stratosphere in 2012, reaching a maximum speed of around 1,357 kilometers per hour (843 miles per hour), or about Mach 1.25, he did not actually break the sound barrier. He came close, but the speed of sound at the altitude he jumped from was lower than at sea level.

To break the sound barrier, an object would need to generate sufficient thrust and have a streamlined design to overcome the aerodynamic challenges associated with supersonic flight. Simply free falling from space, even from the edge of the Earth's atmosphere, would not provide the necessary conditions for breaking the sound barrier.

Even with a high-tech spacesuit, jumping from space directly to Earth wouldn't be a safe or feasible way to travel. Here's why:

Speed: Achieving escape velocity (11.2 km/s) with just a jump is far beyond human capability, even with advanced suit technology. The suit itself wouldn't withstand the immense forces involved, putting the astronaut at risk of injury or worse.

Friction and Heat: Entering Earth's atmosphere at such a high speed would generate tremendous heat, exceeding the suit's heat shield capabilities. The astronaut would face scorching temperatures and potential suit failure.

Controlled Descent: Without a spacecraft or other controlled descent vehicle, the high-speed fall wouldn't allow for a safe landing. Even with advanced suit features, the impact on landing would likely be fatal due to the immense force.

Space Environment: While a spacesuit protects from the vacuum and radiation of space, jumping wouldn't be practical. Astronauts rely on spacecraft for navigation, oxygen and resource replenishment, and communication, all crucial for a safe journey.

Instead of risking a dangerous jump, space travel relies on carefully planned missions using specialized spacecraft. These vehicles employ rockets for propulsion, heat shields for atmospheric entry, and controlled descent systems for safe landings. Astronauts undergo rigorous training to manage these complex systems and operate in the harsh space environment.

So, while venturing into space holds incredible possibilities, there's a reason why professional astronauts follow established procedures and rely on advanced technology. Jumping from space, even with a suit, is simply not a safe or viable option with our current technology and understanding of space travel.

*Common Comments*

• *Why does Earth look so small ?* What you see in the background is not Earth, it is a distorted view of New Mexico and portions of its neighboring states.

• *Where are the stars ?* Stars are rarely ever seen in the daytime, and depending on exposure settings cameras can have a difficult time capturing dimly lit objects in the background while there are much brighter objects in the foreground.

• *Where did all of the water go ?* From that altitude there are no observable oceans within range of the skydiver.

• *Earth is flat* Actually, it isn't - that there are no observable oceans is an indisputable demonstration of the Earth's spherical shape.

• *We shouldn't see that much curvature* The footage is recorded with cameras that are equipped with a wide-angle ( fisheye ) lens which makes the horizon appear curved. The Earth's curvature however can be observed - it is the reason why only New Mexico and portions of its neighboring states are visible in this footage, with the rest of the surface obscured beyond the horizon.

• *Why did they use a wide-angle ( fisheye ) lens ?* The lens is used to obtain a wider field of view.

• *This is fake* Actually, it isn't - the use of space in the title however may seem misleading to some people.

• *How did he fall but the pod stayed in the air ?* One is attached to a helium balloon, the other is not.

• *This isn't real, he should have burned up* The skydiver is neither high enough nor fast enough for re-entry to be an issue.

• *He can't fall in space because there's no gravity* Gravity can, and does, exist in space. The skydiver however jumped from an altitude of 39 kilometers ( not space ) but if he had jumped above the Karman Line ( 100 kilometers ) he would still fall towards Earth because of its gravitational pull.

• *Earth is supposedly rotating but i dont see it moving ?* Earth's equatorial rotation is approximately 1K mph ( and slower than that near Roswell NM ) it may seem fast but in terms of RPM it moves way too slow to notice.

• *How could he possibly land near the same place he left with the Earth moving ?* The capsule / balloon and skydiver rotate with the Earth so it's basically just a matter of going up then coming down - not a whole lot more to it than that.

• *NASA this ... NASA that ...* NASA had nothing to do with this event.

• *What movie is this ?* The clip is not from a movie. This is an edited version of the Red Bull Stratos livestream event from more than a decade ago.

To the people calling this fake, please do more research. Felix was not moving fast enough to burn like a meteor because he did not start off as far out like a meteor does, in fact Felix was technically not even high enough to be considered "in space". He was 125k feet, aka at the lower line of the stratosphere(50km). Meteors come from the mesosphere which is 262k feet(80km) making them fall from a way way way bigger height than where felix was, therefore BURNING. 
The karman line (which is where space starts) is at 100km high. Felix was technically not in space, but he still did what he did, there was no cgi and yes this is completely real and logical and actually possible.

He didn’t burn in the atmosphere because he wasn’t high or fast enough. He was about a third of the way to the boundary with space. And he was only going a maximum of 833mph. Whereas shuttles or capsules experience burning upon reentry at around 17,500mph.

At the point of the jump, most of them have done their jobs: weather data, telemetry, team coordination, life support monitoring, etc. They all have legitimate roles.

The reason baumgartner was not separated from his landing point by earth’s rotation is because he never stopped rotating with the earth. This isn't a supersonic free-fall.  It's damn cool - don't get me wrong, but the speed of sound is not constant.  The reason sound travels is by disturbing particles in the air.  Something disturbs a particle, and that particle interacts with the particle next to it, and so forth.  The higher up you go, the farther and farther those particles are from each other, so it takes longer for them to interact with each other - hence the "speed of sound" gets slower and slower the higher up you go.  So going "supersonic" (meaning faster than sound) is a lot easier when you are 100,000 feet high because the speed of sound at that altitude is much slower because the particles are much farther apart.
